title: The Longevity Benefits of Homeownership: Evidence From Early Twentieth-Century US Male Birth Cohorts
abstract: Owning a home has long been touted as a key com po nent of the ide alized Amer i can Dream. Homeownership is asso ci ated with greater wealth and bet ter health, but the causal impact of homeownership on health remains unclear. Using linked disparities in homeownership rates and pro duce the first U.S.-based esti ma tes of the asso ci a tion between homeownership in early adult hood and lon gev ity. I then use a sib ling-based iden ti fi ca tion strat egy to esti mate the causal effect of homeownership on lon gev ity for cohorts born in the first two decades of the twen ti eth cen tury. The results indi cate that homeownership has a sig nifi cant pos i tive impact on lon gev ity, which I estimate at approximately 4 months.
